design_philosophy.core_loop: Engage enemies with blocking-dependent combat, manage positioning and desperation attacks, progress through stages gaining weapon/armor upgrades that increase power and unlock new abilities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Blocking mechanics create tactical risk/reward—successful blocks grant invincibility windows and counter-attack opportunity, but holding block without incoming strikes leaves player vulnerable
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a legendary knight of Arthurian legend (Arthur, Lancelot, or Perceval), progressively equip stronger weapons and armor, overcome increasingly powerful foes through skill mastery
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Capcom's philosophy emphasizes defensive skill over pure offense: blocking is not optional but vital—some bosses are only vulnerable after their attacks are successfully blocked. This inverts typical beat 'em up power fantasy, rewarding patience and read-ahead timing over button mashing. Character asymmetry (speed vs. strength tradeoff) forces playstyle choice.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Three characters with distinct difficulty ramps: Lancelot (fastest, weakest—beginner-friendly through maneuverability), Arthur (balanced), Perceval (strongest but slowest—intermediate to advanced). Blocking mastery gates progression; execution-heavy desperation attacks trade health for screen clear, creating risk management decisions.

progression.upgrade_system: Automatic level advancement system granting weapon and armor progression. Excalibur grows stronger, characters gain layered armor (chain mail → silver → gold for Arthur; blue tabard + yellow cross → plate armor for Lancelot; light armor + green pants → heavier bare-chest armor for Perceval). Lancelot gains broader sabre; Perceval becomes bald/bearded with progression visual changes.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Level-gated progression through stage completion. Horse-riding sections unlock contextually within stages.
replayability_hooks: Three character archetypes with distinct move sets and difficulty curves encourage replay with different playstyles
Hidden level select (debug feature) enables stage selection
Disabled 'PLAYER DEATH' option mode suggests developmental complexity that could support alternate challenge modes

world_building_techniques: Arthurian legend framing (King Arthur pulling Excalibur, Merlin sending knights to overthrow Garibaldi and unite Britain)
Character archetypes establish narrative identity (Arthur the chosen one, Lancelot the wandering swordsman seeking a worthy king, Perceval the blacksmith's son with gentleness)
Visual progression through armor tiers reinforces character growth and journey

boss_design.philosophy: Boss vulnerability is tied to player defensive mastery—blocking opponent attacks creates invincibility windows and counter-attack opportunities. Some bosses require successful blocks to become vulnerable, shifting player mindset from aggression to defensive read-ahead. Horse-mounted boss variants add environmental complexity.
notable_bosses: Stage bosses with attack patterns requiring blocking to expose vulnerabilities
Horse-mounted boss variants that can ride and attack from horseback

faction_indicators: King Arthur (chosen king, destiny-driven, balanced heroic archetype)
Sir Lancelot (wandering mercenary seeking worthy leadership, mobility-focused, appealing to speed-runners)
Sir Perceval (blacksmith lineage, surprising gentleness despite warrior strength, appeals to power-players comfortable with slow execution)
danger_signals: Blocking mechanic failure = rapid vulnerability windows
Desperation attacks cost health—high-risk commitment in low-health scenarios
Boss invulnerability phases lock progression until blocking conditions are met
environmental_storytelling: Britain unification quest against evil king Garibaldi establishes territory control narrative
Horseback combat sections shift environmental context from ground-based to mounted warfare
Character armor visual tiers telegraph progression and earned status within the world

sources_used: Wikipedia: Knights of the Round (video game) - Gameplay, Characters, Ports sections
The Cutting Room Floor: Knights of the Round (SNES) - Hidden Option Mode, Debug features
